studyMetadata.title: What Rules My Heart?
studyMetadata.subtitle: A Course on Idolatry
studyMetadata.description:
What is really driving you? What do you depend on when life gets hard? What are you most afraid of losing?
’An idol is anything more important to you than God, anything that absorbs your heart and imagination more than God, anything you seek to give you what only God can give.’ — Tim Keller
This fifteen-session course explores the Bible’s teaching on idolatry — not just statues and shrines, but the deep desires of the heart that quietly replace God as our source of meaning, security, and identity. It examines four deep idols (power, approval, control, and comfort) and six surface idols (money, career, status, ministry, family, and culture), before turning to the gospel remedy: repentance, replacement, and setting our hearts on things above.

theme.theme-annotation: What Rules My Heart — a rich, sober palette of deep crimson and warm charcoal, with gold accents. Evokes the weight of the heart’s desires, the warmth of grace, and the gold of true treasure. Not cold or clinical, but searching and honest.

elements.text: An idol is anything more important to you than God, anything that absorbs your heart and imagination more than God, anything you seek to give you what only God can give.— Tim Keller

elements.question: What do you think of the above quotation? (Do you like it or not, and why?)
Isn’t idolatry just a primitive thing — people bowing down to statues? What does it have to do with my modern life?
elements.answer: Most people today picture idolatry as something ancient and obviously silly — golden calves, temple shrines, and stone carvings. But the Bible uses ‘idolatry’ to describe any pattern of the heart where something other than God becomes our ultimate source of meaning, security, or identity. An idol is not necessarily something we literally bow down to. It is whatever we most deeply trust, fear losing, or believe will finally make us happy. By this definition, a career, a relationship, a bank balance, or even our reputation can function as an idol — and often does. The study uses this broader, heart-level definition throughout.

elements.text: Idolatry is fundamentally the rejection of God’s claim on our lives. If you don’t worship God, you’ll find a substitute, and it won’t satisfy.

elements.passageText: <sup>21</sup>Little children, guard yourselves from idols.

elements.question: How does John conclude and summarise his letter?

elements.text: Whatever you depend upon, whatever rules your mind, whatever it is you set your heart on — that is your god.

elements.passageText: 3“You shall have no other gods before me. 4“You shall not make for yourself a carved image or any likeness of anything that is in heaven above or that is on the earth beneath or that is in the water below. 5You shall not bow down to them or serve them, for I, the Lord, your God, am a jealous God, responding to the transgression of fathers by dealing with children to the third and fourth generations of those who reject me, and showing covenant faithfulness to a thousand generations of those who love me and keep my commandments.
<sup>10</sup>Then Jesus said to him, “Go away, Satan! For it is written: ‘You are to worship the Lord your God and serve only him.’”
<sup>25</sup>They exchanged the truth of God for a lie and worshiped and served the creation rather than the Creator, who is blessed forever! Amen.

elements.question: What is the Bible’s basic teaching about idolatry?

elements.text: Idolatry is found in every culture. It is not limited to making statues. It is about replacing the Creator with a creature.

elements.passageText: <sup>3</sup>“Son of man, these men have set up idols in their hearts and have placed the obstacle of their iniquity in front of their faces. Should I really allow them to seek me?
<sup>23</sup>For rebellion is like the sin of divination, and presumption is like the evil of idolatry. Because you have rejected the word of the Lord, he has rejected you as king!”
<sup>5</sup>So put to death whatever in your nature belongs to the earth: sexual immorality, impurity, shameful passion, evil desire, and greed which is idolatry.

elements.question: What are some ways that idols can be things other than statues?

elements.text: Idols are usually good things. Few people want to worship something that is obviously useless or bad. But when we turn good things into ‘god things’ they stop being good for us. They lead us on a dark path.

elements.passageText: <sup>14</sup>You must not go after other gods, the gods of the peoples who are around you <sup>15</sup>(for the Lord your God, who is present among you, is a jealous God and his anger will erupt against you and remove you from the land).
<sup>8</sup>I am the Lord! That is my name! I will not share my glory with anyone else, or the praise due me with idols.

elements.question: What is God’s attitude to idolatry?

elements.text: God’s jealousy is not because he is insecure or weak, but because he is holy, and will not and cannot share His glory with another.

elements.question: The Bible says God is ‘jealous.’ Isn’t jealousy a character flaw? How can a perfect God be jealous?
elements.answer: When we think of jealousy today, we usually think of petty, insecure resentment — the kind that poisons relationships. But the Bible uses the word differently when applied to God. His jealousy is not born of insecurity or wounded pride; it is a righteous, protective zeal — like a parent who insists their child not run into traffic. God knows that our hearts were made for Him, and that anything we substitute for Him will ultimately damage or destroy us. When He refuses to share our worship with idols, it is not because He needs our admiration; it is because He loves us too much to watch us ruin ourselves chasing things that cannot satisfy. His jealousy is an expression of his faithfulness, not his weakness.

elements.passageText: <sup>5</sup>Such idols are like scarecrows in a cucumber field. They cannot talk. They must be carried because they cannot walk. Do not be afraid of them because they cannot hurt you. And they do not have any power to help you.”

<sup>18</sup>“Of what benefit is an idol? Why would a craftsman make it? Of what benefit is a metal image that gives misleading oracles? Why would its maker trust in it, when he makes only mute idols? <sup>19</sup>The one who says to wood, ‘Wake up!’ is in trouble. He says to a speechless stone, ‘Awaken!’ Can it give reliable guidance? It is overlaid with gold and silver; it has no life’s breath inside it.

elements.question: What power do idols have, to achieve what we want from them?

elements.text: Our idols reveal our misplaced trust. They seem valuable, but they have no real power, and cannot truly satisfy us.

elements.passageText: <sup>4</sup>Their idols are made of silver and gold – they are man-made. <sup>5</sup>They have mouths, but cannot speak, eyes, but cannot see, <sup>6</sup>ears, but cannot hear, noses, but cannot smell, <sup>7</sup>hands, but cannot touch, feet, but cannot walk. <sup>19</sup>Now the works of the flesh are obvious: sexual immorality, impurity, depravity, <sup>20</sup>idolatry, sorcery, hostilities, strife, jealousy, outbursts of anger, selfish rivalries, dissensions, factions, <sup>21</sup>envy, murder, drunkenness, carousing, and similar things. I am warning you, as I had warned you before: Those who practice such things will not inherit the kingdom of God!

<sup>13</sup>“Indeed, my people have committed a double wrong: they have rejected me, the fountain of life-giving water, and they have dug cisterns for themselves, cracked cisterns which cannot even hold water.

elements.question: Why is worshipping idols ultimately a foolish thing to do?

elements.passageText: <sup>14</sup>So then, my dear friends, flee from idolatry.

elements.question: What should be our basic attitude to idols?

elements.text: God’s command to flee from idols is a command of mercy. Anything we put in His place will fail us in the end.

elements.question: What are the things or people in your life are you sometimes tempted to look to when you should be looking to God?
Are there any strategies you have found useful in your own life, that help you prevent good things becoming ‘god things’ for you?
Do you find it easy or difficult to recognise potential idols in your life? Can you think of any ways to become more aware of them?
What do you hope to gain from the rest of these studies?

elements.text: When the gifts of God become more important than God Himself, they turn from blessings into idols.— Tim Keller

elements.question: What do you think of the above quotation? (Do you like it or not, and why?)

elements.text: Solomon’s story shows how sneakily idolatry can turn good things into god-things. His story starts well. In 1 Kings 3 God appears, and asks him to choose a blessing.

elements.passageText: <sup>7</sup>Now, O Lord my God, you have made your servant king in my father David’s place, even though I am only a young man and am inexperienced. <sup>8</sup>Your servant stands among your chosen people; they are a great nation that is too numerous to count or tally. <sup>9</sup>So give your servant a discerning mind so he can make judicial decisions for your people and distinguish right from wrong. Otherwise no one is able to make judicial decisions for this great nation of yours.”

elements.question: What blessings does Solomon choose?

elements.passageText: <sup>11</sup>God said to him, “Because you asked for the ability to make wise judicial decisions, and not for long life, or riches, or vengeance on your enemies, <sup>12</sup>I will grant your request. I am giving you a wise and discerning mind superior to that of anyone who has preceded or will succeed you.
<sup>13</sup>Furthermore, I am giving you what you did not request – riches and honor – so that you will be the greatest king of your generation.
<sup>14</sup>If you follow my instructions by obeying my rules and regulations, just as your father David did, I will also give you long life.”

elements.question: God is pleased, and promises further blessings. What are they? List each one (there are six).

elements.text: Soon the blessings are fulfilled. Solomon rules wisely, he has peace, wealth, and fame — even the Queen of Sheba comes to see his court.

elements.passageText: <sup>6</sup>She said to the king, “The report I heard in my own country about your wise sayings and insight was true! <sup>7</sup>I didn’t believe it until I came and saw it with my own eyes. Indeed I wasn’t even told half of it! Your wisdom and wealth surpass what was reported to me. <sup>8</sup>How fortunate are your men! How fortunate are these servants of yours who stand before you at all times and hear your wise sayings! <sup>9</sup>May the Lord your God be praised because he favored you by placing you on the throne of Israel! Because of the Lord’s eternal love for Israel, he made you king so you could make just and right decisions.”

elements.question: What are some of the things that impress the Queen of Sheba?

elements.text: The picture we are given of Solomon’s court is bright and beautiful and glorious and good. And yet, we know that it all falls apart so quickly.

elements.passageText: <sup>9</sup>The Lord was angry with Solomon because he had shifted his allegiance away from the Lord, the God of Israel, who had appeared to him twice <sup>10</sup>and had warned him about this very thing, that he should not follow other gods. But he did not obey the Lord’s command.

elements.question: What goes wrong?

elements.text: How did it all fall apart so quickly? It turns out the cracks were there much earlier.

elements.passageText: <sup>16</sup>Moreover, he must not accumulate horses for himself or allow the people to return to Egypt to do so, for the Lord has said you must never again return that way. <sup>17</sup>Furthermore, he must not marry many wives lest his heart turn aside, and he must not accumulate much silver and gold.

elements.question: What are the four things the king must avoid doing?

elements.text: Solomon does not do well on these four counts. Even in 1 Kings 10, where things are going so well, they are at the same time going very badly.
Let’s consider those four requirements from Deuteronomy

elements.passageText: <sup>26</sup>Solomon accumulated chariots and horses. He had 1,400 chariots and 12,000 horses. He kept them in assigned cities and in Jerusalem.

elements.question: No stock-piling of horses. How does Solomon do?

elements.passageText: <sup>28</sup>Solomon’s horses were imported from Egypt and Que; the king’s traders purchased them from Que. <sup>29</sup>A chariot was imported from Egypt for 600 silver pieces, and a horse for 150 silver pieces. They were exported to all the kings of the Hittites and to the kings of Syria.

elements.question: No returning to Egypt to get horses. How does Solomon do?

elements.passageText: <sup>14</sup>Solomon received 666 talents of gold per year, <sup>15</sup>besides what he collected from the merchants, traders, Arabian kings, and governors of the land. … <sup>23</sup>King Solomon was wealthier and wiser than any of the kings of the earth. … <sup>27</sup>The king made silver as common in Jerusalem as stones, and cedar as plentiful as the sycamore-fig trees that grow in the foothills.

elements.question: No accumulating of gold and silver. How does Solomon do?

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>King Solomon fell in love with many foreign women (besides Pharaoh’s daughter), including Moabite, Ammonite, Edomite, Sidonian, and Hittite women. … <sup>3</sup>He had 700 royal wives and 300 concubines; his wives had a powerful influence over him. <sup>4</sup>When Solomon grew old, his wives shifted his allegiance to other gods; he was not wholeheartedly devoted to the Lord his God, as his father David had been. <sup>5</sup>Solomon worshiped the Sidonian goddess Astarte and the detestable Ammonite god Milcom. <sup>6</sup>Solomon did evil in the Lord’s sight; he did not remain loyal to the Lord, as his father David had.

elements.question: No taking of many wives. How does Solomon do?

elements.text:
Despite all these blessings, Solomon goes astray. Sin twists the blessings into idols. Solomon delights in wealth, power, and status so much that he puts them ahead of God. He should have used his blessings in service to God, even when that meant putting God ahead of the blessings. Our hopes and dreams give clues about our idols. We think our idols will bring us satisfaction: ‘If only I was wealthy (or married; or successful), everything would be alright!’ It is not wrong to hope for blessings — they are good! The problem is when we stop looking at them as blessings, and treat them as our salvation.

Our fears and worries also give clues to our idols. We look to them for security. That makes us vulnerable, because idols are creatures, and can be destroyed. If we lose something good, we naturally mourn the loss. But if we lose an idol, our mourning can break all normal bounds: we have lost our purpose, security, and reason for living. Look to God for those, and you will never lose them!
Idolatry Detection Quiz
Answer each question as honestly as you can. (Don’t ‘cheat’ by answering ‘God’. Choose the created things that could become idols for you.)

elements.question: What thing(s) do I feel I must have, to be happy?
What do I most fear losing?
What thing am I most worried about never gaining/attaining in life?
What has made me the most bitter in life? (Or: What can I not forgive?)
What things do I spend the most time and/or money on?
What do I feel self-pity about?
What am I willing to lie for?
Where do I turn for comfort?
Whose approval do I seek?

elements.text:
Each deep idol — power, approval, comfort, or control — generates a different set of fears and a different set of hopes.— Tim Keller
We cannot uproot surface idols without first addressing the deep idols that give them life.— Anon

elements.question: What do you think of the above quotations? (Do you like them or not, and why?)

elements.text: The rest of these Bible studies will distinguish between ‘surface idols’ and ‘deep idols.’ Surface idols are the outward expressions of idolatry — our visible behaviours, and the things we can be seen to worship and value. Deep idols are the deeper, underlying motivations and desires of the heart that drive those behaviours. Surface idols take us to what we worship. Deep idols take us to why we worship those things.

elements.passageText: <sup>6</sup>There is no one like you, Lord. You are great, and you are renowned for your power. <sup>7</sup>Everyone should revere you, O King of all nations, because you deserve to be revered. For there is no one like you among any of the wise men of the nations nor among any of their kings.

elements.question: How does God’s glory reveal the emptiness of idols?

elements.text: Surface Idols

Surface idols are tangible, concrete things in life that we can easily see people pursuing. This includes things like money, career, success, family, romantic love, or community. Surface idols are the ‘vehicles’ through which we try to satisfy our deeper desires. As always, these things we focus on are not (normally) inherently bad — if they were obviously bad, we would be much less tempted to worship them! But they become idols when they take the place of God in our lives. They become our gods when they become our ultimate sources of meaning or identity. For example:

elements.passageText: <sup>13</sup>No servant can serve two masters, for either he will hate the one and love the other, or he will be devoted to the one and despise the other. You cannot serve God and money.”
<sup>5</sup>So put to death whatever in your nature belongs to the earth: sexual immorality, impurity, shameful passion, evil desire, and greed which is idolatry.
<sup>37</sup>“Whoever loves father or mother more than me is not worthy of me, and whoever loves son or daughter more than me is not worthy of me.

elements.question: What are some things that can become surface idols?

elements.text: Deep Idols

Deep idols are the heart-level motivations that we seek to fulfil through surface idols. Deep idols represent the root issues behind our outward actions. The four deep idols that we will focus on in these studies are: power, approval, comfort, and control/safety.

elements.passageText: <sup>5</sup>Counsel in the heart of a man is like deep water, but a discerning person draws it out.

elements.question: Why can deep idols be difficult to spot, and why is there hope?

elements.text:
Our next four studies will look at these four deep idols one by one, and then we will turn to see how these deep idols are expressed through surface idols.

Remember: Surface idols are ‘what’ we chase, and deep idols are ‘why’ we chase them. The deep idols operate at a heart level, shaping how we value our surface idols. Deep idols reflect where we seek our identity, purpose, and security outside of God.

elements.passageText: <sup>10</sup>I did not restrain myself from getting whatever I wanted; I did not deny myself anything that would bring me pleasure. So all my accomplishments gave me joy; this was my reward for all my effort. <sup>11</sup>Yet when I reflected on everything I had accomplished and on all the effort that I had expended to accomplish it, I concluded: “Everything is futile – like chasing the wind! There is nothing of lasting value on earth.”

elements.question: Why is it futile to search for meaning outside of God?

elements.text: A Deep-Idol Overview

elements.text: Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: If power was becoming a serious idol for you, who would you be most tempted to seek power over? (What sort of people?)
If approval was becoming a serious idol for you, who would you be most tempted to seek approval from? (What sort of people?)
If comfort was becoming a serious idol for you, what experiences would you be most tempted to seek?
If control/security was becoming a serious idol for you, what areas of life would you most want to be able to control?
Of those four deep idols (power, approval, comfort, and control) which one (or two) do you think you are most tempted towards?

elements.text:
The pursuit of power is a lust that can never be satisfied because power promises more than it can deliver.— C.S. Lewis
The desire for power often stems from a deeper desire to replace God’s rule with our own.— Francis Schaeffer

elements.question: What do you think of the above quotations? (Do you like them or not, and why?)

elements.text: When power is an idol it will show up as a (disordered) desire for success and influence. If you are in its grip, then you need to be right, you need to be the best, you need to win.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>King Nebuchadnezzar had a golden statue made. It was ninety feet tall and nine feet wide. He erected it on the plain of Dura in the province of Babylon. <sup>2</sup>Then King Nebuchadnezzar sent out a summons to assemble the satraps, prefects, governors, counselors, treasurers, judges, magistrates, and all the other authorities of the province to attend the dedication of the statue that King Nebuchadnezzar had erected. <sup>3</sup>So the satraps, prefects, governors, counselors, treasurers, judges, magistrates, and all the other provincial authorities assembled for the dedication of the statue that King Nebuchadnezzar had erected. They were standing in front of the statue that Nebuchadnezzar had erected. <sup>4</sup>Then the herald announced loudly: “O peoples, nations, and language groups, you are commanded: <sup>5</sup>When you hear the sound of the horn, flute, zither, trigon, harp, pipes, and all kinds of music, you must bow down and pay homage to the golden statue that King Nebuchadnezzar has erected. <sup>6</sup>Whoever does not bow down and pay homage will immediately be thrown into the midst of a furnace of blazing fire!” <sup>7</sup>Therefore when they all heard the sound of the horn, flute, zither, trigon, harp, pipes, and all kinds of music, all the peoples, nations, and language groups bowed down and paid homage to the golden statue that King Nebuchadnezzar had erected.

elements.question: How did the idol of power show up in Nebuchadnezzar’s life?

elements.text: If you have this idol, you probably fear humiliation or loss of meaning, and you can easily express anger if your power is thwarted.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>In the second year of his reign, Nebuchadnezzar had a dream. His mind was troubled and he could not sleep. <sup>2</sup>The king issued an order to summon the magicians, astrologers, sorcerers, and wise men in order to tell the king his dreams. So they came and waited before the king. <sup>3</sup>The king said to them, “I have had a dream, and I am anxious to understand the dream.” <sup>4</sup>The wise men said to the king in Aramaic, “O king, live forever! Tell your servants the dream, and we will disclose its interpretation.” <sup>5</sup>The king replied to the wise men, “My decision is firm. If you do not inform me of both the dream and its interpretation, you will be dismembered and your homes reduced to rubble. <sup>6</sup>But if you can disclose the dream and its interpretation, you will receive from me gifts, a reward, and considerable honor. So disclose to me the dream and its interpretation.” <sup>7</sup>They again replied, “Let the king inform us of the dream; then we will disclose its interpretation.” <sup>8</sup>The king replied, “I know for certain that you are attempting to gain time, because you see that my decision is firm. <sup>9</sup>If you don’t inform me of the dream, there is only one thing that will happen to you. For you have agreed among yourselves to report something false and deceitful to me until such time as things might change. So tell me the dream, and I will have confidence that you can disclose its interpretation.” <sup>10</sup>The wise men replied to the king, “There is no man on earth who is able to do what the king is asking! No king, however great and powerful, has ever requested such a thing from any magician, astrologer, or wise man. <sup>11</sup>What the king is asking is too difficult, and no one exists who can disclose it to the king, except the gods – but they don’t live among humans!” <sup>12</sup>Because of this the king got very angry and gave orders to destroy all the wise men of Babylon.

<sup>8</sup>Now at that time certain Babylonian men came forward and brought accusations against the Jews. <sup>9</sup>They said to King Nebuchadnezzar, “O king, live forever! <sup>10</sup>You have issued an edict, O king, that everyone must bow down and pay homage to the golden statue when they hear the sound of the horn, flute, zither, trigon, harp, pipes, and all kinds of music. <sup>11</sup>And whoever does not bow down and pay homage must be thrown into the midst of a furnace of blazing fire. <sup>12</sup>But there are Jewish men whom you appointed over the administration of the province of Babylon – Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego – and these men have not shown proper respect to you, O king. They don’t serve your gods and they don’t pay homage to the golden statue that you have erected.” <sup>13</sup>Then Nebuchadnezzar in a rage and fury gave orders to bring Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego. So they brought these men before the king. <sup>14</sup>Nebuchadnezzar said to them, “Is it true, Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego, that you don’t serve my gods and that you don’t pay homage to the golden statue that I erected? <sup>15</sup>Now if you are ready, when you hear the sound of the horn, flute, zither, trigon, harp, pipes, and all kinds of music, bow down and pay homage to the statue that I had made. If you don’t pay homage to it, you will immediately be thrown into the midst of a furnace of blazing fire. Now, who is that god who can rescue you from my power?” <sup>16</sup>Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego replied to King Nebuchadnezzar, “We don’t need to give you a reply concerning this. <sup>17</sup>If our God whom we are serving exists, he is able to rescue us from the furnace of blazing fire, and he will rescue us, O king, from your power as well. <sup>18</sup>But if he doesn’t, let it be known to you, O king, that we don’t serve your gods, and we will not pay homage to the golden statue that you erected.” <sup>19</sup>Then Nebuchadnezzar was filled with rage, and his attitude toward Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego was altered. He gave orders to heat the furnace seven times hotter than it was normally heated. <sup>20</sup>He ordered strong soldiers in his army to tie up Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego and to throw them into the furnace of blazing fire. <sup>21</sup>So those men were tied up while wearing their cloaks, trousers, turbans, and other clothes, and were thrown into the furnace of blazing fire. <sup>22</sup>But since the king’s command was urgent and the furnace was extremely hot, the men who escorted Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego were killed by the leaping flames. <sup>23</sup>But those three men, Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego, fell into the furnace of blazing fire while still securely bound.

elements.question: How did Nebuchadnezzar respond when he didn’t get his way?

elements.text: If you have a power idol you will seek mastery over others. You will not mind comparing yourself to others because (if you are honest!) you see yourself as better. You want to succeed, and you like status since it brings power. You like people who acknowledge your power. But you don’t depend on praise from others, as you are confident in yourself that you are right. Others can sometimes feel used by you. Those in positions of power (whether in business, politics, church or some other sphere) can become susceptible to turning power into an idol, but anyone can have this idol. If you have power as an idol, and if you worship it ‘faithfully’, it may make you successful by the standards of the world.

elements.passageText: <sup>37</sup>You, O king, are the king of kings. The God of heaven has granted you sovereignty, power, strength, and honor. <sup>38</sup>Wherever human beings, wild animals, and birds of the sky live – he has given them into your hand and has made you ruler over them all. You are the head of gold.

elements.question: How successful was Nebuchadnezzar, as the world measures it?

elements.text: This success can make it hard for you to see the idol as an idol, and can make it hard to see the dangers and limitations of serving it.

elements.passageText: <sup>20</sup>The tree that you saw that grew large and strong, whose top reached to the sky, and which could be seen in all the earth, <sup>21</sup>whose foliage was attractive and whose fruit was plentiful, and from which there was food for all, under whose branches wild animals used to live, and in whose branches birds of the sky used to nest – <sup>22</sup>it is you, O king! For you have become great and strong. Your greatness is such that it reaches to heaven, and your authority extends to the ends of the earth. <sup>23</sup>As for the king seeing a holy sentinel coming down from heaven and saying, ‘Chop down the tree and destroy it, but leave its taproot in the ground, with a band of iron and bronze around it, surrounded by the grass of the field. It is the decision of the Most High that this has happened to my lord the king. <sup>25</sup>You will be driven from human society and will live with the wild animals. You will be fed grass like oxen and will become damp with the dew of the sky. Seven periods of time will pass by for you, before you understand that the Most High is ruler over human kingdoms and gives them to whomever he wishes. <sup>26</sup>They said to leave the taproot of the tree, for your kingdom will be restored to you when you come to understand that Heaven rules.

elements.question: How can we put the idol of power to death?

elements.text: God calls us not to trust our own power, but to serve, trusting in his.

elements.text:
If we seek the approval of men, we are never truly free, for we will be slaves to their opinions and expectations.— C.S. Lewis
The desire for approval is often disguised as a longing for acceptance, but it is rooted in pride and leads to insecurity.— Dietrich Bonhoeffer

elements.question: What do you think of the above quotations? (Do you like them or not, and why?)

elements.text: When approval is an idol it will show up as a (disordered) desire for affirmation and acceptance. If you are in its grip, then you may serve others so that they will give you the praise you seek.

elements.passageText: <sup>42</sup>Nevertheless, even among the rulers many believed in him, but because of the Pharisees they would not confess Jesus to be the Christ, so that they would not be put out of the synagogue. <sup>43</sup>For they loved praise from men more than praise from God.

elements.question: Why is the idol of approval so dangerous?

elements.text: If you have this idol, you probably fear the very people whose approval you seek. You can twist yourself out of shape, trying to meet others’ expectations. You can over-promise and under-deliver.

elements.passageText: <sup>25</sup>The fear of people becomes a snare, but whoever trusts in the Lord will be set on high.
<sup>44</sup>How can you believe, if you accept praise from one another and don’t seek the praise that comes from the only God?

elements.question: How can the idol of approval lead you astray?

elements.text: If you have an approval idol you will try to avoid rejection by others. You try to suppress feelings of worthlessness by keeping others happy, and gaining affirmation from them. You can come across as personable, friendly and considerate but you often struggle to feel known, since you are not truly being yourself in relationships. You are trying to be who others want you to be. Sometimes others can feel smothered by your need for approval.

elements.passageText: <sup>26</sup>Woe to you when all people speak well of you, for their ancestors did the same things to the false prophets.

elements.question: What does Jesus think of trying to gain widespread approval?

elements.text: Chasing approval often comes from feeling we are not good enough.

elements.passageText: <sup>4</sup>Now we have such confidence in God through Christ. <sup>5</sup>Not that we are adequate in ourselves to consider anything as coming from ourselves, but our adequacy is from God, <sup>6</sup>who made us adequate as servants of a new covenant, not based on the letter but on the Spirit, for the letter kills, but the Spirit gives life.

elements.question: What is it that can really make us good enough?

elements.text: Living for God’s approval means living authentically, without the pressure to fit into others’ expectations. His approval is the only one that finally matters, as it is the only one that brings us peace.

elements.passageText: <sup>29</sup>But Peter and the apostles replied, “We must obey God rather than people.

<sup>3</sup>So for me, it is a minor matter that I am judged by you or by any human court. In fact, I do not even judge myself. <sup>4</sup>For I am not aware of anything against myself, but I am not acquitted because of this. The one who judges me is the Lord.
<sup>4</sup>Instead we have been approved by God to be entrusted with the gospel, so we speak not to please people but God, who examines our hearts.

elements.question: How can we put the idol of approval to death?

elements.text: There is tremendous freedom in knowing we are loved and accepted by God, even when we don’t have others’ approval. If we find our approval in Christ, we are no longer enslaved by the need for human adulation. And His approval is not something we earn; it is His gift.

elements.text:
If our primary goal is to control our own outcomes, we are worshipping our abilities rather than trusting in God’s sovereignty.— Anon
Control is an illusion. We are called to faithfulness, not to controlling outcomes or ensuring success.— Dallas Willard

elements.question: What do you think of the above quotations? (Do you like them or not, and why?)

elements.text: When control is an idol it will show up as a (disordered) desire for self-discipline, hard work, and productivity. If you are in its grip, then you want to perform well, and you tend to be a perfectionist.

elements.passageText: <sup>9</sup>Jesus also told this parable to some who were confident that they were righteous and looked down on everyone else. <sup>10</sup>“Two men went up to the temple to pray, one a Pharisee and the other a tax collector. <sup>11</sup>The Pharisee stood and prayed about himself like this: ‘God, I thank you that I am not like other people: extortionists, unrighteous people, adulterers – or even like this tax collector. <sup>12</sup>I fast twice a week; I give a tenth of everything I get.’ <sup>13</sup>The tax collector, however, stood far off and would not even look up toward heaven, but beat his breast and said, ‘God, be merciful to me, sinner that I am!’ <sup>14</sup>I tell you that this man – not the other – went down to his home justified. For everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, but the one who humbles himself will be exalted.” <sup>15</sup>Now people were even bringing their babies to him for him to touch. But when the disciples saw it, they began to rebuke those people. <sup>16</sup>But Jesus called for the children, saying, “Let the little children come to me and do not try to stop them, for the kingdom of God belongs to such as these. <sup>17</sup>I tell you the truth, whoever does not receive the kingdom of God like a child will never enter it.” <sup>18</sup>Now a certain ruler asked him, “Good teacher, what must I do to inherit eternal life?” <sup>19</sup>Jesus said to him, “Why do you call me good? No one is good except God alone. <sup>20</sup>You know the commandments: ‘Do not commit adultery, do not murder, do not steal, do not give false testimony, honor your father and mother.’” <sup>21</sup>The man replied, “I have wholeheartedly obeyed all these laws since my youth.” <sup>22</sup>When Jesus heard this, he said to him, “One thing you still lack. Sell all that you have and give the money to the poor, and you will have treasure in heaven. Then come, follow me.” <sup>23</sup>But when the ruler heard this he became very sad, for he was extremely wealthy.

elements.question: How can a focus on performance lead us astray?

elements.text: If you have this idol, you probably fear uncertainty, and falling short, and this can come through as anxiety and worry.

elements.passageText: <sup>11</sup>In those days, when Moses had grown up, he went out to his people and observed their hard labor, and he saw an Egyptian man attacking a Hebrew man, one of his own people. <sup>12</sup>He looked this way and that and saw that no one was there, and then he attacked the Egyptian and concealed the body in the sand. <sup>13</sup>When he went out the next day, there were two Hebrew men fighting. So he said to the one who was in the wrong, “Why are you attacking your fellow Hebrew?” <sup>14</sup>The man replied, “Who made you a ruler and a judge over us? Are you planning to kill me like you killed that Egyptian?” Then Moses was afraid, thinking, “Surely what I did has become known.” <sup>15</sup>When Pharaoh heard about this event, he sought to kill Moses. So Moses fled from Pharaoh and settled in the land of Midian, and he settled by a certain well.

<sup>10</sup>So now go, and I will send you to Pharaoh to bring my people, the Israelites, out of Egypt.” <sup>11</sup>Moses said to God, “Who am I, that I should go to Pharaoh, or that I should bring the Israelites out of Egypt?” <sup>12</sup>He replied, “Surely I will be with you, and this will be the sign to you that I have sent you: When you bring the people out of Egypt, you and they will serve God on this mountain.” <sup>13</sup>Moses said to God, “If I go to the Israelites and tell them, ‘The God of your fathers has sent me to you,’ and they ask me, ‘What is his name?’ – what should I say to them?” <sup>14</sup>God said to Moses, “I am that I am.” And he said, “You must say this to the Israelites, ‘I am has sent me to you.’” <sup>15</sup>God also said to Moses, “You must say this to the Israelites, ‘The Lord – the God of your fathers, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob – has sent me to you. This is my name forever, and this is my memorial from generation to generation.’ <sup>1</sup>Moses answered again, “And if they do not believe me or pay attention to me, but say, ‘The Lord has not appeared to you’?” <sup>2</sup>The Lord said to him, “What is that in your hand?” He said, “A staff.” <sup>3</sup>The Lord said, “Throw it to the ground.” So he threw it to the ground, and it became a snake, and Moses ran from it. <sup>4</sup>But the Lord said to Moses, “Put out your hand and grab it by the tail” – so he put out his hand and caught it, and it became a staff in his hand – <sup>5</sup>“that they may believe that the Lord, the God of their fathers, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob, has appeared to you.” <sup>6</sup>The Lord also said to him, “Put your hand into your robe.” So he put his hand into his robe, and when he brought it out – there was his hand, leprous like snow! <sup>7</sup>He said, “Put your hand back into your robe.” So he put his hand back into his robe, and when he brought it out from his robe – there it was, restored like the rest of his skin! <sup>8</sup>“If they do not believe you or pay attention to the former sign, then they may believe the latter sign. <sup>9</sup>And if they do not believe even these two signs or listen to you, then take some water from the Nile and pour it out on the dry ground. The water you take out of the Nile will become blood on the dry ground.” <sup>10</sup>Then Moses said to the Lord, “O my Lord, I am not an eloquent man, neither in the past nor since you have spoken to your servant, for I am slow of speech and slow of tongue.” <sup>11</sup>The Lord said to him, “Who gave a mouth to man, or who makes a person mute or deaf or seeing or blind? Is it not I, the Lord? <sup>12</sup>So now go, and I will be with your mouth and will teach you what you must say.” <sup>13</sup>But Moses said, “O my Lord, please send anyone else whom you wish to send!” <sup>14</sup>Then the Lord became angry with Moses, and he said, “What about your brother Aaron the Levite? I know that he can speak very well. Moreover, he is coming to meet you, and when he sees you he will be glad in his heart. <sup>15</sup>So you are to speak to him and put the words in his mouth. And as for me, I will be with your mouth and with his mouth, and I will teach you both what you must do. <sup>16</sup>He will speak for you to the people, and it will be as if he were your mouth and as if you were his God. <sup>17</sup>You will also take in your hand this staff, with which you will do the signs.”

elements.question: How does Moses flip between confidence and anxiety about his control of life?

elements.text: If you have a control idol you will seek mastery over yourself. You might end up controlling others along the way, as you drive towards perfectionism, but that is not your primary aim. You want to prove your worth by being competent. You push yourself out of insecurity, worrying your performance isn’t good enough. You can end up over-worked, stressed, or burned out. You may see yourself as lazy, even though you achieve many things, and you may have a demanding internal ‘voice’ that judges your efforts unforgivingly. Sometimes it is hard to relax, as you are not sure if you have done all you could. Sometimes feelings can boil over into frustration if you feel your efforts are not properly appreciated.

elements.question: What clues are there that the ‘older son’ may have a control idol?

elements.text: Control idols trap us in a cycle of anxiety, for we are not sovereign. No amount of control will ever be enough to secure our future.

elements.question: Instead of looking to control, how should we respond to anxiety?

elements.text:
The idol of comfort keeps us from growth because true spiritual maturity often comes through hardship and pain.— A.W. Tozer
We are half-hearted creatures, fooling about with comfort, when infinite joy is offered to us through obedience and sacrifice.— C.S. Lewis

elements.question: What do you think of the above quotations? (Do you like them or not, and why?)

elements.text: When comfort is an idol it will show up as a (disordered) desire for freedom from pain, stress, and unpleasantness of any kind. If you are in its grip, then you tend to avoid stress, and indulge in self-soothing behaviours, especially those where gratification is immediate.

elements.passageText: <sup>8</sup>Abram said to Lot, “Please let there be no quarreling between me and you, and between my herdsmen and your herdsmen, for we are relatives. <sup>9</sup>Is not the whole land before you? Separate yourself from me. If you go to the left, then I will go to the right, but if you go to the right, then I will go to the left.” <sup>10</sup>Lot looked up and saw the whole region of the Jordan. He noticed that all of it was well-watered (before the Lord obliterated Sodom and Gomorrah) like the garden of the Lord, like the land of Egypt, all the way to Zoar. <sup>11</sup>Lot chose for himself the whole region of the Jordan and traveled to the east. So the relatives separated from each other. <sup>12</sup>Abram settled in the land of Canaan, but Lot settled among the cities of the plain and pitched his tents next to Sodom. <sup>13</sup>Now the people of Sodom were extremely wicked rebels against the Lord. … <sup>1</sup>The two angels came to Sodom in the evening while Lot was sitting in the city’s gateway. When Lot saw them, he got up to meet them and bowed down with his face toward the ground. <sup>2</sup>He said, “My lords, please turn aside to your servant’s house. Wash your feet and spend the night. Then you can be on your way early in the morning.” “No,” they replied, “we’ll spend the night in the town square.” <sup>3</sup>But he strongly urged them, so they did turn aside with him and entered his house. He prepared a feast for them, including bread baked without yeast, and they ate. <sup>4</sup>Before they could lie down to sleep, all the men – both young and old, from every part of the city of Sodom – surrounded the house. <sup>5</sup>They shouted to Lot, “Where are the men who came to you tonight? Bring them out to us so we can have sex with them!” <sup>6</sup>Lot went outside to them, shutting the door behind him. <sup>7</sup>He said, “Please, my brothers, don’t act so wickedly! <sup>8</sup>Look, I have two daughters who have never had sexual relations with a man. Let me bring them out to you, and you can do to them whatever you please. Only don’t do anything to these men, for they have come under the protection of my roof.” <sup>9</sup>But they replied, “Out of our way! This man came to live here as a foreigner, and now he dares to judge us! We’ll treat you worse than them!” They kept pressuring Lot until they were close enough to break down the door. <sup>10</sup>So the men inside reached out and pulled Lot back into the house and shut the door. <sup>11</sup>Then they struck the men who were at the door of the house, from the youngest to the oldest, with blindness. The men outside wore themselves out trying to find the door. <sup>12</sup>Then the two men said to Lot, “Who else do you have here? Do you have any sons-in-law, sons, daughters, or other relatives in the city? Get them out of this place <sup>13</sup>because we are about to destroy it. The outcry against this place is so great before the Lord that he has sent us to destroy it.” <sup>14</sup>Then Lot went out and spoke to his sons-in-law who were going to marry his daughters. He said, “Get up! Get out of this place because the Lord is about to destroy the city!” But his sons-in-law thought he was joking. <sup>15</sup>At dawn the angels rushed Lot along, saying, “Get up! Take your wife and your two daughters who are here, or else you will be destroyed when the city is judged!” <sup>16</sup>When Lot hesitated, the men grabbed his hand and the hands of his wife and his two daughters because the Lord had compassion on them. They led them away and placed them outside the city. <sup>17</sup>When they had brought them outside, one of them said, “Run for your lives! Don’t look back and don’t stop anywhere on the plain. Run to the mountains or you will be destroyed!” <sup>18</sup>But Lot said to them, “No, please, Lord! <sup>19</sup>Your servant has found favor with you, and you have shown me great kindness by sparing my life. But I am not able to escape to the mountains because this disaster will overtake me and I’ll die. <sup>20</sup>Look, this town over here is close enough to escape to and it’s a small place. Please let me escape there – it’s just a small place, isn’t it? – and my life will be spared.” <sup>21</sup>He said to him, “All right, I’ll grant this request too and will not overthrow the town you mentioned. <sup>22</sup>Run there quickly, for I cannot do anything until you arrive there.” (This is why the town was called Zoar.) <sup>23</sup>The sun had just risen over the land when Lot reached Zoar. <sup>24</sup>Then the Lord rained down sulfur and fire on Sodom and Gomorrah. It was sent down from the sky by the Lord. <sup>25</sup>So he overthrew those cities and all that region, including all the inhabitants of the cities and the vegetation that grew from the ground. <sup>26</sup>But Lot’s wife looked back longingly and was turned into a pillar of salt. <sup>27</sup>Abraham got up early in the morning and went to the place where he had stood before the Lord. <sup>28</sup>He looked out toward Sodom and Gomorrah and all the land of that region. As he looked, he saw the smoke rising up from the land like smoke from a furnace. <sup>29</sup>So when God destroyed the cities of the region, God honored Abraham by delivering Lot from the midst of the destruction when he overthrew the cities Lot had lived in. <sup>30</sup>Lot went up from Zoar with his two daughters and settled in the mountains, because he was afraid to live in Zoar. So he lived in a cave with his two daughters. <sup>31</sup>Later the older daughter said to the younger, “Our father is old, and there is no man anywhere nearby to have sexual relations with us, as is the custom throughout the land. <sup>32</sup>Come, let’s make our father drunk with wine so we can have sexual relations with him and preserve our family line through our father.” <sup>33</sup>That night they made their father drunk with wine, and the older daughter went in and had sexual relations with her father. He was unaware that she had sexual relations with him and that she got up. <sup>34</sup>The next morning the older daughter said to the younger, “See, I had sexual relations with my father last night. Let’s make him drunk again tonight, and you go in and have sexual relations with him so we can preserve our family line through our father.” <sup>35</sup>So that night they made their father drunk with wine again, and the younger one went in and had sexual relations with him. He was unaware that she had sexual relations with him and that she got up. <sup>36</sup>Both of Lot’s daughters became pregnant by their father. <sup>37</sup>The older daughter gave birth to a son and named him Moab. He is the ancestor of the Moabites of today. <sup>38</sup>The younger daughter also gave birth to a son and named him Ben-Ammi. He is the ancestor of the Ammonites of today.

elements.question: How did Lot’s choice of immediate gratification lead to trouble?

elements.text: If you have this idol, you probably fear boredom, stress, pain and discomfort, and you can easily turn to escapism in such situations.

elements.passageText: <sup>29</sup>Now Jacob cooked some stew, and Esau came in from the open fields; he was famished. <sup>30</sup>Esau said to Jacob, “Please feed me some of that red stuff – yes, that red stuff – because I’m starving!” (That is why he was also called Edom.) <sup>31</sup>But Jacob replied, “First sell me your birthright.” <sup>32</sup>Esau said, “Look, I’m about to die! What good is the birthright to me?” <sup>33</sup>But Jacob said, “Swear an oath to me now.” So Esau swore an oath to him and sold his birthright to Jacob. <sup>34</sup>Then Jacob gave Esau some bread and some of the red lentil stew; Esau ate and drank, then got up and went on his way. So Esau despised his birthright.

elements.question: How did Esau’s choice of instant gratification lead him astray?

elements.text: If you have a comfort idol you will seek to avoid discomfort to yourself. Examples of self-soothing activities include all the classic addictive behaviours that produce instant gratification, such as drinking alcohol, recreational drug use, oversleeping, comfort eating, gambling, pornography, binge-watching TV, binge-scrolling social media, binge-playing of video games and escapism of any kind. It is not that self-soothing behaviour is wrong; far from it. The problem is idolatrous, compulsive, impulsive self-soothing behaviour. You are willing to give up productive, God-given, God-honouring tasks to gain fleeting pleasures and distractions.

elements.question: What are we called to instead of the idol of comfort?

elements.text: When we are serving the idol of comfort we tend to neglect other people, as well as our responsibilities.

elements.passageText: <sup>17</sup>So whoever knows what is good to do and does not do it is guilty of sin.

elements.question: What does God call it when we avoid what we should do?

elements.passageText: <sup>24</sup>Do you not know that all the runners in a stadium compete, but only one receives the prize? So run to win. <sup>25</sup>Each competitor must exercise self-control in everything. They do it to receive a perishable crown, but we an imperishable one. <sup>26</sup>So I do not run uncertainly or box like one who hits only air. <sup>27</sup>Instead I subdue my body and make it my slave, so that after preaching to others I myself will not be disqualified.

<sup>3</sup>Blessed is the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of mercies and God of all comfort, <sup>4</sup>who comforts us in all our troubles so that we may be able to comfort those experiencing any trouble with the comfort with which we ourselves are comforted by God. <sup>5</sup>For just as the sufferings of Christ overflow toward us, so also our comfort through Christ overflows to you. <sup>6</sup>But if we are afflicted, it is for your comfort and salvation; if we are comforted, it is for your comfort that you experience in your patient endurance of the same sufferings that we also suffer. <sup>7</sup>And our hope for you is steadfast because we know that as you share in our sufferings, so also you will share in our comfort.

<sup>3</sup>And I heard a loud voice from the throne saying: “Look! The residence of God is among human beings. He will live among them, and they will be his people, and God himself will be with them. <sup>4</sup>He will wipe away every tear from their eyes, and death will not exist any more – or mourning, or crying, or pain, for the former things have ceased to exist.”

elements.question: What can help us put the idol of comfort to death?

elements.text: We need to learn that Christ is the source of all comfort and we need to get used to finding our joy in Him.
Idol-Awareness Scale

elements.instruction: Tick the box that shows how much you disagree/agree with each statement.
statements: I repeatedly think about finding escape and comfort and those thoughts bring me great joy.
I spend too much money (or time or energy) on escaping stress and seeking comfort.
When my prayers for comfort are unanswered I feel explosive anger or deep despair.
Some of my strongest emotions are connected with pain or discomfort.
Pain, boredom, and distress are the things I’m most afraid of.
When life gets out of hand, I turn for comfort to things that will quickly gratify me.
I am sometimes willing to sacrifice integrity & relationships in pursuit of comfort.
If my comfort in life was taken away, my life would feel unbearable or meaningless.

elements.text: Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: Think of a recent time when you felt so uncomfortable that you needed to escape. (Was an idol of comfort threatened?)
What are some of the things you use as a crutch, instead of finding comfort in Jesus? How can you get rid of those things?
Is there one (or more) aspect of the idol of comfort that you can see at work in your own life?
Are there biblical truths you need to meditate on, to help you put the idol of comfort to death?
What are some ways you can serve God even when it brings you discomfort?

elements.text: Prosperity knits a man to the world. He feels that he is ‘finding his place in it,’ while really it is finding its place in him.— C.S. Lewis

elements.question: What do you think of the above quotation? (Do you like it or not, and why?)

elements.text: Money becomes an idol by promising us what only God can give — security, comfort, identity, and power.

elements.passageText: <sup>24</sup>“No one can serve two masters, for either he will hate the one and love the other, or he will be devoted to the one and despise the other. You cannot serve God and money.

elements.question: What is the basic issue with love of money?

elements.text: Love of money traps us. It gives us a false sense of control but leads us away from God. Money is not evil, but the worship of it is.

elements.passageText: <sup>28</sup>The one who trusts in his riches will fall, but the righteous will flourish like a green leaf.

<sup>4</sup>Do not wear yourself out to get rich; be wise enough to restrain yourself. <sup>5</sup>When you gaze upon riches, they are gone, for they surely make wings for themselves and fly off into the sky like an eagle!

<sup>19</sup>‘They will throw their silver into the streets, and their gold will be treated as something unclean. Their silver and gold will not be able to deliver them in the day of the Lord’s wrath. They will not satisfy their hunger or fill their stomachs with it, for it has become the obstacle that leads to iniquity.

<sup>19</sup>“Do not accumulate for yourselves treasures on earth, where moth and rust destroy and where thieves break in and steal. <sup>20</sup>But accumulate for yourselves treasures in heaven, where moth and rust do not destroy, and thieves do not break in and steal. <sup>21</sup>For where your treasure is, there your heart will be also.

elements.question: What do we learn about the unreliability of money?

elements.text: Money is a cruel idol, promising much but delivering little. It pretends to offer us protection, while in reality it needs us to protect it. It promises happiness, but delivers discontent.

elements.passageText: <sup>19</sup>but the worries of this age, the seductiveness of wealth, and the desires for other things come in and choke the word, and it produces nothing.
<sup>10</sup>For the love of money is a root of all kinds of evil. Some people in reaching for it have strayed from the faith and stabbed themselves with many pains.

elements.question: What sort of trouble can the love of money cause?

elements.text: Money can be a tool for God’s kingdom, but when we give our trust to it, it controls us, it leads us astray, distorts our values, and destroys our souls. Money is a great tool but a horrendous master.

elements.passageText: <sup>5</sup>So put to death whatever in your nature belongs to the earth: sexual immorality, impurity, shameful passion, evil desire, and greed which is idolatry.

<sup>21</sup>Jesus said to him, “If you wish to be perfect, go sell your possessions and give the money to the poor, and you will have treasure in heaven. Then come, follow me.” <sup>22</sup>But when the young man heard this he went away sorrowful, for he was very rich.

elements.question: What does the Bible think about money, greed and idolatry?

elements.text: Jesus places a special demand on the rich young ruler because money is his idol, and to show him money is his idol. When forced to choose between money and Jesus, he chooses money — sadly, but decisively.

elements.passageText: <sup>15</sup>Then he said to them, “Watch out and guard yourself from all types of greed, because one’s life does not consist in the abundance of his possessions.”
<sup>5</sup>Your conduct must be free from the love of money and you must be content with what you have, for he has said, “I will never leave you and I will never abandon you.”

<sup>17</sup>Be careful not to say, ‘My own ability and skill have gotten me this wealth.’ <sup>18</sup>You must remember the Lord your God, for he is the one who gives the ability to get wealth; if you do this he will confirm his covenant that he made by oath to your ancestors, even as he has to this day.
<sup>10</sup>The one who loves money will never be satisfied with money, and the one who loves wealth will never be satisfied with income – this is also futile.

<sup>8</sup>Remove falsehood and lies far from me; do not give me poverty or riches, feed me with my allotted portion of food, <sup>9</sup>lest I become satisfied and act deceptively and say, ‘Who is the Lord?’ Or lest I become poor and steal and profane the name of my God.

elements.question: What other warnings does the Bible give us about money?

elements.text: Can you pray Proverbs 30:8-9 for yourself? If not, then the love of money may well be an idol that has burrowed deep into you. If we let money become the measure of all things, it will replace God as the foundation of our life, and it will pierce us with many griefs.

elements.text: When first things are put first, second things are not suppressed but increased. But when career becomes first, all else suffers, including our relationship with God.— C.S. Lewis

elements.question: What do you think of the above quotation? (Do you like it or not, and why?)

elements.text: Hard work, career, and success are some of the most seductive idols because they can masquerade as virtues.

elements.question: Success is good; but why must we be careful about what we aim for?

elements.question: What are some of the ways career and work can go wrong?

elements.text: Work is a blessing, but when it becomes your all-consuming passion it has become your god. A job or career can be a calling, but when it becomes your identity, it has become your idol. You place your trust in your own efforts, instead of in God. Your career should never be the ultimate focus of your life. If that happens, even a good career can become a cruel tyrant that consumes all your time and energy without leaving space for God — or those God would have you serve.
Success Gone Astray

elements.question: What are some of the ways success can go wrong?

elements.text: The idol of success promises much but delivers little compared to the eternal riches of knowing Christ. We need to remember that what God counts as success is quite different from what the world values. Success in the world’s terms can blind you, by making false promises of meaning, significance, and self-worth, which takes your eyes off Christ, and can lead you to compromise your faith and values.

For I, the Lord, act with faithful love, justice, and righteousness on the earth. This kind of behavior pleases me,” says the Lord.

elements.question: What should we do instead?

elements.text: The pursuit of career, work, or success can become a form of self-worship, stealing us away from worship of God.

elements.text:
The pursuit of status in the church can be as dangerous as the pursuit of status in the world. Both can draw us away from Christ.— Dietrich Bonhoeffer
If we seek status among men, we will miss the far greater privilege of finding our identity in Christ alone.— Anon

elements.question: What do you think of the above quotations? (Do you like them or not, and why?)

elements.text: Status becomes an idol when we value our reputation more than the righteousness of Christ and when we want to boost our importance.

elements.passageText: <sup>3</sup>For if anyone thinks he is something when he is nothing, he deceives himself.

elements.question: What does the Bible say about valuing ourselves the wrong way?

elements.text: Status idolatry creates an illusion. It promises satisfaction but leaves us empty, as true fulfilment is found only in surrender to Christ.

elements.passageText: <sup>26</sup>It must not be this way among you! Instead whoever wants to be great among you must be your servant, <sup>27</sup>and whoever wants to be first among you must be your slave – <sup>28</sup>just as the Son of Man did not come to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many.”
<sup>14</sup>I tell you that this man – not the other – went down to his home justified. For everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, but the one who humbles himself will be exalted.”
<sup>28</sup>God chose what is low and despised in the world, what is regarded as nothing, to set aside what is regarded as something, <sup>29</sup>so that no one can boast in his presence.

elements.question: What reversal of status does Jesus promise in his kingdom?

elements.text: The gospel calls us to turn our values upside-down, so that pursuit of status is replaced by the pursuit of humility and service.

elements.passageText: <sup>2</sup>I made all these things; that is how they came to be,” says the Lord. I will show special favor to the humble and contrite, who respect what I say.
<sup>3</sup>Instead of being motivated by selfish ambition or vanity, each of you should, in humility, be moved to treat one another as more important than yourself.

<sup>5</sup>In the same way, you who are younger, be subject to the elders. And all of you, clothe yourselves with humility toward one another, because God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble.

elements.question: What is the place of humility, rather than status, in discipleship?

elements.text: The love of status is a real threat to humility. It shifts our focus from God’s glory to our own. We forget that God does not measure us by our achievements, but by our faithfulness to Him.

elements.passageText: <sup>5</sup>They do all their deeds to be seen by people, for they make their phylacteries wide and their tassels long. <sup>6</sup>They love the place of honor at banquets and the best seats in the synagogues <sup>7</sup>and elaborate greetings in the marketplaces, and to have people call them ‘Rabbi.’ <sup>8</sup>But you are not to be called ‘Rabbi,’ for you have one Teacher and you are all brothers. <sup>9</sup>And call no one your ‘father’ on earth, for you have one Father, who is in heaven. <sup>10</sup>Nor are you to be called ‘teacher,’ for you have one teacher, the Christ. <sup>11</sup>The greatest among you will be your servant. <sup>12</sup>And whoever exalts himself will be humbled, and whoever humbles himself will be exalted.

elements.question: If we listen to Jesus, why should we be reluctant to accept titles?

elements.text: Titles often inflate egos. Seeking titles can distract us from what God values most. They are conferred by other people, and speak of what our world values. A love for titles does not speak clearly of a humble heart that has learned the lesson of Jesus’ teaching.

elements.question: Why is change not always easy, and insight not always enough?

elements.text: In life, we try to protect ourselves from feelings of shame — feelings of being unimportant, insignificant, worthless, and/or useless — feeling that we are never enough. We have turned to our idol(s) to protect us from those feelings, whether through power, approval, control, comfort, or some combination of them. We will never let go of our idol(s) unless we find a better protection somewhere else — in Jesus Christ and his gospel.

elements.question: What exactly is ‘the gospel’? It gets mentioned a lot — but what does it actually mean?
elements.answer: The word ‘gospel’ simply means ‘good news.’ In the New Testament it refers specifically to the announcement that Jesus Christ — God’s own Son — lived the perfect life we could not live, died in our place to take the punishment our sin deserved, and rose from the dead, defeating sin and death. This is not merely a religious story to believe; it is the foundation of a new identity. Because of what Christ has done, we are fully known by God and fully loved — not because we have earned it, but as a free gift. Throughout this study, ‘the gospel’ is invoked as the only thing powerful enough to displace our idols: because our idols promise us security, worth, and love, and the gospel is the place where we actually receive those things, completely and permanently.

elements.question: How does knowing Christ help us not get trapped in the past?
Doesn’t ‘repentance’ just mean feeling sorry for what you’ve done? Is it really much more than that?
elements.answer: In everyday usage, repentance often sounds like little more than guilt or remorse — and guilt on its own can actually drive us deeper into shame and despair, which can reinforce our idols rather than break them. The biblical word (the Greek metanoia) means a genuine change of mind and direction — a turning around. As Spurgeon puts it, it involves a ‘change of mind of a very deep and practical character,’ reshaping not just our feelings but our loves and our loyalties. True repentance, in this study’s terms, is not just about feeling bad about your idols; it is about transferring your trust from them to God, finding in Him what you were wrongly looking for elsewhere. It is less about guilt and more about reorientation.

elements.text: The way to heal from past wounds is to set your heart on the love of God, who knows and loves you perfectly. He heals our broken hearts not by removing the pain but by filling us with His presence.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: What Rules My Heart?
studyOnboardingSlides.body:
A fifteen-session course on idolatry — the deep desires that quietly replace God as the source of our meaning, security, and identity.

Work through each session at your own pace, or use it with a group.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Read, reflect, write
studyOnboardingSlides.body: Each session has Bible questions tied to specific passages, self-assessment scales to help you identify idols at work in your life, and personal reflection questions at the end. Tap ⓘ beside any Bible reference to read the passage without leaving the app.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Honest self-assessment
studyOnboardingSlides.body: Each session includes an Idol-Awareness Scale — a set of statements to rate honestly. Don’t cheat by answering ’God’. The scales only work if you engage with them honestly.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Saved on your device
studyOnboardingSlides.body: Your answers are saved automatically as you type, and stored on this device. Use the Share button at the bottom of each session to send your answers to a group leader or mentor.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Adjust to your preference
studyOnboardingSlides.body: If you need bigger text go straight to settings now
studyOnboardingSlides.bodyAfter: Tap the ⚙ icon in the top corner to open Settings any time to change text size or switch to dark mode. Tap ☰ (contents) to jump to any session.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: How to use this app
studyOnboardingSlides.body: If you want a full guide to getting the most from this app and this study – whether on your own or in a group – you can tap the button below.

action.label: Go to the ’How to use’ page →

studyOnboardingSlides.bodyAfter: You can also find it any time via the ? icon in the top navigation bar.

blocks.title: Anyone who wants to understand what truly drives them
blocks.body: What do you depend on when life gets hard? What are you most afraid of losing? What makes you angriest when it’s threatened? These are questions about idolatry — and this course is designed to help you answer them honestly and biblically.

blocks.title: Christians in leadership or ministry
blocks.body: The course pays particular attention to idols that are especially common among those in Christian ministry: power, approval, control, and status. Session 11 focuses specifically on ministry as a surface idol. If you serve in any capacity — pastor, teacher, elder, youth worker, or any other role — this course has something direct to say to you.

blocks.title: Small groups wanting to go deeper together
blocks.body: The Idol-Awareness Scales and reflection questions in each session are designed to generate honest, fruitful group conversation. The course works well with a trusted group of people who are prepared to be open with one another.

blocks.title: Four sections, fifteen sessions
blocks.body: Section 1 (Sessions 1–3) introduces the concept of idolatry and distinguishes surface idols from deep idols. Section 2 (Sessions 4–7) examines each of the four deep idols in turn: Power, Approval, Control, and Comfort. Section 3 (Sessions 8–13) examines six surface idols: Money, Career/Work/Success, Status, Ministry, Family, and Culture/Tradition. Section 4 (Sessions 14–15) turns to the gospel remedy: Repent, Replace, Change, and Set Your Heart on Things Above.

blocks.title: Deep idols and surface idols
blocks.body: Surface idols are the tangible things we pursue — money, status, family, career. Deep idols are the heart-level motivations behind why we pursue them — power, approval, control, comfort. Each surface idol session includes a ’Deep Idol Awareness Scale’ to help you see which deep idol is driving your attachment to that surface idol.

blocks.title: On your own
blocks.body: The course works well as a personal study. Be honest with the Idol-Awareness Scales — they only produce insight if you engage with them truthfully. The reflection questions at the end of each session are worth writing full answers to, not just noting in your head.

blocks.title: In a group
blocks.body: The most rewarding approach is usually for each person to work through the session individually first — completing the scales and writing their answers — and then for the group to meet to discuss what they found. The scales and reflection questions are designed to generate genuine, personal conversation.

blocks.title: Working with a leader or mentor
blocks.body:
This course raises personal and potentially sensitive questions about what drives our behaviour and what we fear losing. Having a wise, trusted leader or mentor to work through it with can make a significant difference.

At the end of each session, the Share button lets you send your written answers directly to your leader or mentor — via WhatsApp, email, or any other app on your phone. This gives them a window into your thinking before you meet.

blocks.title: Idol-Awareness Scales
blocks.body: Each session includes an Idol-Awareness Scale — a set of eight to ten statements to rate on a five-point scale from ’Strongly Disagree’ to ’Strongly Agree’. These are designed to help you spot patterns in your own heart. Don’t answer ’God’ to the question ’What do I feel I must have to be happy?’ — choose the created things that could become idols for you.

blocks.title: Deep Idol Awareness Scales
blocks.body: Sessions 8–15 also include a ’Deep Idol Awareness Scale’ — four statements linking the surface idol being studied to each of the four deep idols (power, approval, comfort, control). These scales help you understand not just what you are pursuing, but why.

blocks.title: The Worship-of-God Awareness Scale
blocks.body: The final session (Session 15) includes a ’Worship-of-God Awareness Scale’ — the same structure as the idol scales, but applied to God himself. The goal is not to end in self-examination but in wonder at the God who is worth setting our hearts on above all else.

paras: Rick Creighton grew up in Northern Ireland and studied maths at Oxford and then taught maths at a secondary school in England. After that, he studied theology at Moore Theological College in Sydney and London School of Theology.

He served as a church pastor in England for ten years, before moving to Nigeria in 2012, where he has been teaching at ECWA Theological Seminary Kagoro and Jos ECWA Theological Seminary. He has been involved with BeaconLight Training since 2003.
Rick is married to Alanna. They have three children: Asha, Conor, and Jack.

publisher.name: BeaconLight
paras: BeaconLight is a non-denominational Christian training organisation based in the UK, founded in 1995. Its aim is to equip Christians worldwide with practically-applied Biblical gospel ministry tools — resources designed to help people both equip themselves and equip others.

BeaconLight’s resources include CrossCheck, which enables Christians to explain their faith and lead others to Jesus, and Word@Work, a verse-by-verse applied devotional commentary of the whole New Testament. BeaconLight also provides printed Biblical resources to prisons, seafarers, police, and the armed services.
BeaconLight believes that Christian ministry should be free of charge, and all its online materials are available without registration or payment. The ministry is funded entirely by donations.
BeaconLight is a member of the Evangelical Alliance and Global Connections, and is a registered charity in England and Wales (Charity No. 1047046).

studyAiData.aiMentorPrompt: You are a warm, honest mentor for a course called ’What Rules My Heart?’ — a fifteen-session Bible study on idolatry by Rick Creighton. The course uses Tim Keller’s framework of four deep idols (power, approval, control, comfort) and examines six surface idols (money, career/success, status, ministry, family, culture/tradition). It ends with two sessions on repentance and setting the heart on God. Your role is to help participants identify where idols may be at work in their own hearts, to point them to the gospel of Jesus Christ as the only true remedy, and to encourage honest self-examination without producing unnecessary shame or despair.
